chapter_summary: Ruru, a descendant of Bhrigu, grieves bitterly in the forest over his betrothed Pramadvara, the daughter of a gandharva and an apsara, who lies dead. He laments and offers the merit of his austerities and service to his elders to revive her. A devaduta (divine messenger) appears and tells him that the life of one whose lifespan is spent cannot be restored by mere words, but that the gods have ordained a means: if Ruru gives half of his own lifespan to the maiden, she will rise. Ruru consents, surrendering half his life. The gandharva king and the devaduta then approach Dharmaraja (Yama), who grants that Pramadvara rise endowed with half of Ruru's lifespan. She rises as if waking from sleep. Their parents joyfully conduct the marriage on an auspicious day, and the couple delights in one another. Having won his rare bride, Ruru takes a vow to destroy all crawling serpents, and thereafter strikes them dead wherever he finds them. One day, entering a great forest, he sees an aged Dundubha (a non-venomous snake) lying there and raises his death-like staff to kill it. The Dundubha protests, asking why Ruru, in his fury, would slay it when it has committed no offense against him.

story.title: Ruru Revives Pramadvara and Vows Vengeance on Serpents
story.arc: Grief drives Ruru to bargain half his own life to revive his dead beloved Pramadvara; the gods and Dharmaraja grant it and the couple weds, but his unhealed rage over her death (caused by a serpent) hardens into a vow to exterminate all snakes, which collides with an innocent Dundubha who pleads its blamelessness.
story.timeline_note: Early in the Adi Parva, within the frame of Sauti narrating the lineage of Ruru; precedes the Dundubha's recounting of the Khandava/Sahasrapada tale.
story.comic_potential: The mighty serpent-slayer Ruru, staff raised mid-swing, frozen by a talking snake calmly asking 'what exactly did I do to you?' — righteous fury meeting a reasonable question.

teachings.teaching: Ruru offers the accumulated merit of his lifelong austerities and devoted service to his elders as the price for reviving his beloved, treating tapas as a transferable spiritual currency.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework, the energy stored through sustained sadhana (dhrtavrata, steadfast vow) is real and conserved; here it is spent outward in love rather than inward toward liberation, showing the seeker still bound to attachment.

teachings.teaching: A divine messenger teaches that words alone cannot restore one whose allotted lifespan is exhausted; the dead do not return by grief, for there is no remaining life in the lifeless.
teachings.lens_note: The lesson that ayus (allotted life-energy) is finite and grief cannot reverse its exhaustion mirrors the Kriya teaching that lifespan is a measured store of prana; only its conscious redirection, not lamentation, alters destiny.

teachings.teaching: The remedy ordained by the gods is sacrificial: only by surrendering half of his own lifespan can Ruru raise Pramadvara, so that her renewed life is literally carved from his.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads such exchanges as the law that life-force given is life-force lost from the giver; spiritual gain for another is paid in one's own pranic account, never created from nothing.

teachings.teaching: Unresolved grief curdles into indiscriminate violence: having recovered his bride, Ruru turns his steadfastness into a vow to slay every serpent, striking even those that never harmed him.
teachings.lens_note: In the Kriya lens, the same dhrtavrata discipline that could liberate becomes destructive when yoked to unmastered rage; the unpurified emotion, not the vow itself, makes the sadhaka a danger.
